code: @Manual{,   title = {bssdm: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling},   author = {Sean Haythorne and James Camac and John Baumgartner},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.2.2},   url = {https://github.com/cebra-analytics/bssdm}, }

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: bssdm package provides Species Distribution Modelling (SDM) implementations two presence-methods predicting spatial distribution environmental suitability exotic pests, diseases, biosecurity threats, utilising spatial species occurrence records corresponding environmental variables. two SDM methods : Range bagging (Drake, 2015) Climatch (ABARES, 2020) package also provides two tools assessing environmental novelty projection area relative species occurrence locations used build model: MESS — Multivariate Environmental Similarity Surface (Elith et al., 2010) ExDet — Extrapolation Detection (Mesgaran et al., 2014)

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: can install latest version bssdm GitHub :
code: remotes::install_github("cebra-analytics/bssdm")

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: following example generates environmental suitability Hawkweed species (Hieracium pilosella), exotic weed Australia, using bssdm package Range bagging Climatch method implementations.

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: SDM requires spatial environmental data form GeoTIFF raster layers area interest, encapsulating species occurrence records (build model), well locations predicted environmental suitability desired. use selection global climate data WorldClim (Fick & Hijmans, 2017; http://www.worldclim.org). required use bssdm package, use geodata package download global climate data example. geodata package can installed : climate data can downloaded :
code: install.packages("geodata") climate_rast_all <- geodata::worldclim_global(   var = "bio", res = 10, path = tempdir() ) # Climate WorldClim (BIOCLIM) data # BIO02: Mean Diurnal Range # BIO05; Max Temperature of Warmest Month # BIO11: Mean Temperature of Coldest Quarter # BIO12: Annual Precipitation # BIO14: Precipitation of Driest Month # BIO15: Precipitation Seasonality (Coefficient of Variation) climate_rast <- climate_rast_all[[c(2,5,11,12,14,15)]] climate_rast #> class       : SpatRaster  #> size        : 1080, 2160, 6  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) #> resolution  : 0.1666667, 0.1666667  (x, y) #> extent      : -180, 180, -90, 90  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) #> coord. ref. : lon/lat WGS 84 (EPSG:4326)  #> sources     : wc2.1_10m_bio_2.tif   #>               wc2.1_10m_bio_5.tif   #>               wc2.1_10m_bio_11.tif   #>               ... and 3 more sources #> names       : wc2.1~bio_2, wc2.1~bio_5, wc2.1~io_11, wc2.1~io_12, wc2.1~io_14, wc2.1~io_15  #> min values  :     1.00000,   -29.68600,   -66.31125,           0,           0,      0.0000  #> max values  :    21.14754,    48.08275,    29.15299,       11191,         484,    229.0017

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: SDM requires species occurrence records specified table latitude longitude coordinates using WGS84 coordinate reference system (CRS). use global Hawkweed (Hieracium pilosella) occurrences downloaded Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF, 2026) cleaned CoordinateCleaner (Zizka et al., 2019) remove duplicates incomplete incorrect records.
code: library(bssdm)  # Cleaned Hawkweed (Hieracium pilosella) occurrences (see ?hawkweed for details) head(hawkweed) #>         lon      lat verified #> 1  7.999168 44.85531        1 #> 2 14.782217 50.90555        1 #> 3 12.676944 50.66167        1 #> 4 12.431944 50.68472        1 #> 5 12.483333 50.70000        1 #> 6 12.506383 50.73638        1  # Plot BIOCLIM BIO02 & occurrences terra::plot(climate_rast[[1]], colNA = "grey",             main = "BIOCLIM Mean Diurnal Range & Hawkweed occurrences (red)",             xlab = "Longitude (degrees)", ylab = "Latitude (degrees)") terra::plot(terra::vect(hawkweed, crs = "EPSG:4326"),             col = "red", pch = 20, alpha = 0.5, add = TRUE)

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: run SDM first build model, use predict suitability area interest using climate data matching variables. Although climate data may differ extent, CRS, resolution, time frame (e.g. past future climate), reuse climate data used build model. build models predict suitability Range bagging Climatch SDM methods.

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
code: # Run Range bagging SDM rangebag_model <- bssdm::rangebag(   climate_rast, hawkweed, parallel_cores = 1 ) rangebag_output <- predict(rangebag_model, climate_rast, raw_output = FALSE) # Plot the Range bagging SDM predicted climate suitability terra::plot(rangebag_output, colNA = "grey",             main = "Range bagging SDM predicted climate suitability",             xlab = "Longitude (degrees)", ylab = "Latitude (degrees)")

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
code: # Run Climatch SDM climatch_model <- bssdm::climatch(   climate_rast, hawkweed, parallel_cores = 1 ) climatch_output <- predict(climatch_model, climate_rast, raw_output = FALSE) # Plot the Climatch SDM predicted climate suitability terra::plot(climatch_output, colNA = "grey",             main = "Climatch SDM predicted climate suitability",             xlab = "Longitude (degrees)", ylab = "Latitude (degrees)")

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: Correlative SDMs identify statistical relationships occurrence patterns spatial environmental data estimate suitability location given environment. models powerful relationships estimated one region (e.g., species’ native range) can used infer suitability another region interest. However, projection region may environmental conditions represented model-fitting data — either variable outside model-fitting range, multiple variables combine produce novel conditions — leading model extrapolation. Model extrapolation can reduce prediction reliability, important understand spatial distribution novel environmental conditions model outputs can interpreted appropriately. bssdm provides two tools purpose: ExDet (Mesgaran et al., 2014) MESS (Elith et al., 2010). functions require reference dataset: climate values extracted species occurrence locations used build model.
code: # Extract climate values at occurrence locations (reference data for novelty assessment) ref_data <- terra::extract(climate_rast,                            terra::vect(hawkweed, crs = "EPSG:4326"),                            ID = FALSE)

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: Multivariate Environmental Similarity Surface (MESS; Elith et al., 2010) quantifies similar location’s environmental conditions reference (occurrence) data. Positive values indicate conditions within reference range; negative values indicate novel (dissimilar) conditions. mess() function also returns dissimilar variable (MoD) similar variable (MoS) location.
code: # Calculate MESS mess_output <- bssdm::mess(climate_rast, ref_data, full = TRUE) plot(mess_output) plot(mess_output, which = "mod")

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling
text: Extrapolation Detection tool (ExDet; Mesgaran et al., 2014) distinguishes two types environmental novelty. Type 1 (univariate) novelty occurs one variables fall outside reference range; Type 2 (multivariate) novelty occurs combination variables unusual even individual variables within range. exdet() function returns score values less 0 indicate Type 1 novelty, values 0 1 indicate analog conditions, values greater 1 indicate Type 2 novelty.
code: # Calculate ExDet with most influential covariates exdet_output <- bssdm::exdet(climate_rast, ref_data, mic = TRUE) plot(exdet_output) plot(exdet_output, which = "mic1") plot(exdet_output, which = "mic2")

title: Biosecurity Species Distribution Modelling

(2014). ‘dragons: tool quantifying novelty due covariate range correlation change projecting species distribution models’. what: Climatch SDM model building method — climatch
title: Climatch SDM model building method — climatch
text: model building component implementation ABARES Climatch species distribution modelling (SDM) method (ABARES, 2020).

title: Climatch SDM model building method — climatch
code: climatch(   x,   p,   algorithm = c("euclidean", "closest_standard_score"),   d_max = 50,   sd_data = NULL,   as_score = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'Raster' climatch(   x,   p,   algorithm = c("euclidean", "closest_standard_score"),   d_max = 50,   sd_data = NULL,   as_score = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'SpatRaster' climatch(   x,   p,   algorithm = c("euclidean", "closest_standard_score"),   d_max = 50,   sd_data = NULL,   as_score = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'SpatVector' climatch(   x,   p,   algorithm = c("euclidean", "closest_standard_score"),   d_max = 50,   sd_data = NULL,   as_score = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' climatch(   x,   p,   algorithm = c("euclidean", "closest_standard_score"),   d_max = 50,   sd_data = NULL,   as_score = TRUE,   ... )

title: Climatch SDM model building method — climatch
text: x Climate (environmental) data terra::SpatRaster, terra::SpatVector, raster::Raster* (CRS), data.frame WGS84 lon lat columns. p Species occurrence data data.frame (matrix) WGS84 lon lat columns. algorithm Climatch method algorithm selected "euclidean" (default) "closest_standard_score". d_max Maximum range distance, kilometres, used matching occurrence points nearest climate data points/cells (default = 50). sd_data Optional data.frame calculating standard deviation climate variable, vector pre-calculated values. as_score Logical indicate whether generate score 0-10 (default = TRUE) values 0-1 (FALSE). ... Additional parameters.

title: Climatch SDM model building method — climatch
text: "Climatch" model S4 object containing slots: method SDM method: "climatch". algorithm Algorithm: "euclidean" "closest_standard_score"). variables List climate (environmental) variable names. sd standard deviation variable calculated via climate data (x) sd_data provided. presence selected (nearest within range) climate data occurrence point. coordinates coordinates selected climate data. as_score Indication whether generate score 0-10 values 0-1.

title: Climatch SDM model building method — climatch

what: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
title: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
text: Detect quantify univariate (Type 1) multivariate (Type 2) environmental novelty projecting species distribution models.

title: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
code: exdet(x, ref, mic = FALSE, filename = "", tol = .Machine$double.eps, ...)  # S3 method for class 'Raster' exdet(x, ref, mic = FALSE, filename = "", tol = .Machine$double.eps, ...)  # S3 method for class 'SpatRaster' exdet(x, ref, mic = FALSE, filename = "", tol = .Machine$double.eps, ...)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' exdet(x, ref, mic = FALSE, tol = .Machine$double.eps, ...)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' exdet(x, ref, mic = FALSE, tol = .Machine$double.eps, ...)

title: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
text: x Climate (environmental) data terra::SpatRaster, raster::Raster*, data.frame, matrix layer/column represents focal values environmental variable. ref data.frame, matrix, list column/element represents reference values environmental variable (corresponding given x). mic Logical indicate whether influential covariates returned. TRUE, function returns variables responsible Type 1 Type 2 novelty. Default FALSE. filename Optional filename writing spatial raster output (.e., exdet raster ). Default "". tol Tolerance value passed mahalanobis() matrix inversion. Default .Machine$double.eps. See ?solve details. ... Additional parameters.

title: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
text: x SpatRaster Raster* object, function returns list containing: exdet: SpatRaster layer giving extrapolation detection scores values < 0 indicate univariate novelty (Type 1), values 0 1 indicate analog conditions, values > 1 indicate novel covariate combinations (Type 2); mic1: factor SpatRaster layer indicating variable influential Type 1 novelty. Value "novel" Type 1 novelty occurs (included mic=TRUE); mic2: factor SpatRaster layer indicating variable influential Type 2 novelty. Value "novel" Type 2 novelty occurs (included mic=TRUE). x data.frame matrix, function return list , single layer SpatRaster objects replaced vectors, factor rasters replaced factor vectors.

title: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
text: exdet implements ExDet (Extrapolation Detection) method described Mesgaran et al. (2014). detects novel univariate ranges (Type 1 novelty) novel combinations covariates (Type 2 novelty) projection area compared reference area. Type 1 novelty assessed comparing environmental variable range reference data. Type 2 novelty assessed using Mahalanobis distance detect novel combinations variables, even individual variables within reference ranges. ExDet score prioritises univariate novelty. one variables outside reference range, Type 2 novelty considered. mic=TRUE, influential covariates (MIC) identified types novelty. MIC1 identifies variable contributes Type 1 novelty (outside reference range). MIC2 identifies variable contributes Type 2 novelty (via leave-one-influence analysis). Areas novelty labeled "novel" MIC outputs.

title: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
text: Mesgaran, M. B., Cousens, R. D., Webber, B. L. (2014). dragons: tool quantifying novelty due covariate range correlation change projecting species distribution models. Diversity Distributions, 20(10): 1147-1159. doi:10.1111/ddi.12209

title: Extrapolation Detection for Species Distribution Models — exdet
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(geodata) library(terra) bio <- worldclim_global("bio", res = 10, path = tempdir()) aus <- gadm("AUS", level = 0, resolution = 2, path = tempdir()) occ <- spatSample(aus, size = 100, method = "random") ref <- terra::extract(bio, occ, ID = FALSE) ex <- exdet(bio, ref, mic = TRUE)  # Plot outputs plot(ex) plot(ex, which = "mic1") plot(ex, which = "mic2") } # }

title: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity — mess
text: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity dissimilar/similar variables respect reference dataset, set environmental variables.

title: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity — mess
code: mess(x, ref, full = FALSE, filename = "", ...)  # S3 method for class 'Raster' mess(x, ref, full = FALSE, filename = "", ...)  # S3 method for class 'SpatRaster' mess(x, ref, full = FALSE, filename = "", ...)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' mess(x, ref, full = FALSE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' mess(x, ref, full = FALSE, ...)

title: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity — mess
text: x Climate (environmental) data terra::SpatRaster, raster::Raster*, list, data.frame, matrix layer/element/column represents focal values environmental variable. ref data.frame, matrix, list column/element represents reference values environmental variable (corresponding given x). full Logical indicate whether similarity values returned variables. FALSE (default), minimum similarity scores across variables returned. filename Optional filename writing spatial raster output (.e., mess raster ). Default "". ... Additional parameters.

title: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity — mess
text: x SpatRaster Raster* object, function returns list containing: mess: SpatRaster layer giving minimum similarity value across variables location (.e. MESS); mess_by_variable: SpatRaster giving environmental similarities variable x (included full=TRUE); mod: factor SpatRaster layer indicating variable dissimilar reference range (.e. MoD map, Elith et al. 2010); mos: factor SpatRaster layer indicating variable similar reference range. x list, matrix data.frame, function return list , multilayer single layer SpatRaster objects replaced matrix vector objects, respectively.

title: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity — mess
text: mess uses MESS algorithm described Appendix S3 Elith et al. 2010.

title: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity — mess
text: Elith, J., Kearney, M., Phillips, S. (2010). art modelling range-shifting species. Methods Ecology Evolution, 1: 330-342. doi:10.1111/j.2041-210X.2010.00036.x

title: Calculate Multivariate Environmental Similarity — mess
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(geodata) library(terra) bio <- worldclim_global("bio", res = 10, path = tempdir()) aus <- gadm("AUS", level = 0, resolution = 2, path = tempdir()) occ <- spatSample(aus, size = 100, method = "random") ref <- terra::extract(bio, occ, ID = FALSE) m <- mess(bio, ref, full = TRUE)  # Plot outputs plot(m) plot(m, which = "mod") plot(m, which = "mos") } # }

code: # S3 method for class 'Climatch' predict(   object,   x,   algorithm = NULL,   sd_data = NULL,   as_score = NULL,   raw_output = NULL,   filename = "",   parallel_cores = NULL,   debug = FALSE,   ... )

title: Climatch SDM predict method — predict.Climatch
text: object "Climatch" model S4 object containing slots: method SDM method: "climatch". algorithm Algorithm: "euclidean" "closest_standard_score". variables List climate (environmental) variable names. sd standard deviation variable calculated via climate data (x) sd_data provided. presence selected (nearest within range) climate data occurrence point. coordinates coordinates selected climate data. as_score Indication whether generate score 0-10 (TRUE) values 0-1 (FALSE). x Climate (environmental) data corresponding model variables terra::SpatRaster, raster::Raster*, data.frame, matrix. algorithm Optional (overriding) Climatch method algorithm selected "euclidean" "closest_standard_score". sd_data Optional (overriding) data.frame calculating standard deviation climate variable, vector pre-calculated values. as_score Optional (overriding) logical indicate whether generate score 0-10 (TRUE) values 0-1 (FALSE). raw_output Logical indicate whether return raw predicted values (TRUE) object (per x: FALSE). Default NULL, returning either raw values spatial raster (per x). filename Optional filename writing spatial raster output (). Default "". parallel_cores Optional number cores available parallel processing, thus enable parallel processing. Default NULL (serial). debug Output additional debug information (memory block info, etc.). Default FALSE. ... Additional parameters.

title: Climatch SDM predict method — predict.Climatch
text: Predicted values raw vector terra::SpatRaster, raster::Raster*, data.frame, matrix (per x).

code: rangebag(   x,   p,   n_models = 100,   n_dim = 2,   sample_prop = 0.5,   limit_occur = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'Raster' rangebag(   x,   p,   n_models = 100,   n_dim = 2,   sample_prop = 0.5,   limit_occur = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'SpatRaster' rangebag(   x,   p,   n_models = 100,   n_dim = 2,   sample_prop = 0.5,   limit_occur = TRUE,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' rangebag(   x,   p,   n_models = 100,   n_dim = 2,   sample_prop = 0.5,   limit_occur = TRUE,   ... )

title: Range bagging SDM model building method — rangebag
text: x Climate (environmental) data terra::SpatRaster raster::Raster* (CRS), data.frame WGS84 lon lat columns. p Species occurrence data data.frame (matrix) WGS84 lon lat columns. points outside extent x ignored. n_models Number convex hull models build sampled environment space (default = 100). n_dim Number dimensions (variables) sampled convex hull models (default = 2). sample_prop Proportion environment data rows sampled fitting convex hull model (default = 0.5). limit_occur Logical indicate whether limit occurrence data one per environment data cell (default = TRUE). ... Additional parameters.

title: Range bagging SDM model building method — rangebag
text: "Rangebag" model S4 object containing slots: method SDM method: "rangebag". variables List climate (environmental) variable names. presence selected climate data corresponding occurrences points. coordinates coordinates selected climate data. ch_models list convex hull models (vertices).
